facts.text: Malaga was named after Málaga, Spain, in the 19th century by early settlers.
The Woodbury‑to‑Glassboro rail line opened in 1861, putting a station half a mile from Malaga’s main street and linking it to Camden, Millville, and Cape May.
In the mid‑1800s, Malaga supported about 60 settler families and boasted a glass factory that employed 100‑200 workers, making it a regional industrial hub.
Malaga sits at 39°34′11″N 75°02′52″W, roughly 95 feet above sea level, and lies about 23 miles southeast of Philadelphia.
Malaga’s landscape features flat uplands with sandy, well‑drained soils like Aura sandy loam, supporting local farms and aiding groundwater recharge.
The Old Malaga Road, surveyed early 19th century, connected Millville to Malaga and helped move lumber, charcoal, and produce to Philadelphia after the War of 1812.
